    E.g. Bertrand, Droit à la vie privée, 137-138. For French struggles over this, see also Elisabeth Logeais
and Jean-Baptiste Schroeder, The French Right of Image : An Ambiguous Concept Protecting the Human
Persona, 18 Loy.L.A.Ent.L.J. 511 (1998).

    Discussion with further references in Solove and Rotenberg, Information Privacy Law, 162-163.

    White v. Samsung Electronics America, Inc., 989 F.2d 512 (9th Cir. 1993). For this complaint, see
Joanthan Kahn, Bringing Dignity Back to Light: Publicity Rights and the Eclipse of the Tort of

254
    See the discussion of Pamela Anderson Lee (along with directions to a relevant internet site!) in
Bertrand, Droit à la vie privée, 163.

79
 Carson’s rights were commercial rights only, not privacy rights against humiliation or

embarrassment. 255 Here again, we can see a contrast with continental law, and with

German law in particular. Even commercial enterprises can be “insulted” in Germany.

German firms have “personality” rights, and they are indeed protected against

embarrassing or humiliating uses of their slogans or logos, through what is called the

doctrine of Markenverunglimpfung.256 Some cases litigated under the American “right of

publicity” cases may come out the same way that they would be in European right to

one’s image cases. But the underlying values are different, and courts feel obliged to say

as much.

Indeed, even if some cases come out the same way, many come out differently.

And there are many areas of the law, as we have seen, where Americans do not even

perceive the sorts of privacy violations that seem to Europeans obviously present. The

Europeans are right. At the end of the day, Americans do not really grasp the European

idea of the protection of privacy.

VII

But does that mean that Americans don’t understand the moral imperative of

privacy in the creation of “personhood”? Such is the conclusion that commentators

repeatedly draw, both in Europe and in the United States. Yet I hope it is clear that the

problem is more complex than that. If Europeans protect “privacy,” it is not because they

255
    Carson v. Here’s Johnny Portable Toilets, Inc., 698 F.2d 831 (6th Cir. 1983); see also Finger v. Omni
Publications International, Ltd., 566 N.E.2d 141 (N.Y. Ct.App. 1990).
256
    See esp. the case known as “Markenverunglimpfung I,” available at
http://www.adicor.de/urteile.nsf/0/f71f118710a2d257c125687a002f3f0e?OpenDocument. French law is
more relaxed on this issue. See the dicussion of Isabelle de Lamberterie and Xavier Strubel, L’Image
Manipulée, in Pascale Bloch, ed., Image et Droit (Paris: L’Harmattan, 2002), 335-374, here 343-344.

80
 understand universal moral truths that Americans fail to understand. It is because they

live in societies that have been shaped by certain kinds of cultural expectations and
